item.title: oof
item.content:encoded:
Very hard to judge, but for me it is a no vote because it does not interest me that much. Pretty innovative though.

The idea itself is pretty cool though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ok dude 10/10 I can just imagine the shark grow huge and eat several people and then shrink so tiny to stay away from anyone trying to hunt it down or research it. It's cool and I think it fits in with the SCP foundation extremely well.

item.title: Ok stop with getting pissing with the littlest of problems.
item.content:encoded: I like it. A shark that can change sizes to up to 14 meters (which is about 45 feet) long which is interesting and fits in with the SCP foundation. Just be more discritive such as instead of labeling the SCP as "Carcharodon carcharias" just use great white shark. As soon as a new SCP is made people crituque it for the smallest of thing, but this SCP really really works. Thumbs up from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: yes exactly

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I mean, it's gone now, so *shrug*

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: https://i.imgur.com/rvk59ob.gif

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I mean, you argue that there's no reason for the SCI to mention 𝒩ness, and I agree, but that doesn't mean the interpretation is unacceptable. I think it's kinda boneheaded to think 𝒩ness has nothing to do with Fisk, but the article doesn't explicitly state the opposite. Maybe there's an objective unit of something's worth, and Fisk was literally objectively worthless? It's a stretch but it's a conclusion you can come to.

Additionally, the interpretation you're showing here directly contradicts the original author's intention.

La mort de l'auteur. Intention doesn't matter all that much.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Really more funny than anything else. The video log feels like something out of an anomalous version of Rob Dyrdek's Ridiculousness, which would be great if the log wasn't essentially the big hook of the whole article.

no vote

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Just barely gets a -1 from me. The supplemental classification itself is harmless enough, but the memo drawing attention to it led me to believe it was going to be more important. Like how you know not all is as it seems when you see an article that starts with "pending reclassification"? Except this was extremely straightforward, and the supplemental classification was just a throwaway detail.

Secondly, the video log doesn't really add anything except a cheap SPC joke, since it's just a simple recounting of the shark's escape. And as a nitpick, it's unnecessary to add that 14 meters is the size of a humpback whale since, after all, the entire point of measurements is to describe the size of things. The fact that 14 meters is a ludicrous size for a shark is already conveyed by the description of it crushing nearby structures.

I like the basic concept, and it sounds like I missed some major improvements. It just needs a little more. And a bloodthirsty size-changing shark could certainly explain why shark movies are still a thing when all you have to do is stay out of the ocean.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Quite honestly, it's not a valid interpretation. There are plenty of concepts and articles on the site that leave lots of room to interpretation and headcanon, but 4517 isn't one of them. There is one interpretation of what 𝒩ness represents, and that is that it is the objective measure of the subjective value1 of a given object relative to the value Damien Pisk placed on himself, at 0. So why would that value be present within the context of SCI? It's completely useless for them to measure except for the purposes of studying that one specific phenomenon. Additionally, the interpretation you're showing here directly contradicts the original author's intention.

Footnotes
1. To Damien Pisk

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I kinda feel like it's a rip-off of the game of Tasty Blue and I personally found the supplemental very useless to the overall story(which overall note evolving into much )
So, in the end, it's a -1 for me

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
To answer your criticisms in order:
1) Valid. Makes sense, I'll change it.
2) The 500 newtons is basically to explain why 4458 grew to the size of a whale: the only thing that *could* break it would be… well, the weight of a whale. Although admittedly 500k newton-proof glass is a bit much.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oi, I really like this one, a few things to crit when you eventually resubmit it.

Following several unsuccessful containment attempts (including Incident 4458-1), all residents of Harmony, Maine were evacuated from the town, relocated to other areas, and administered Class C amnestics to remove all memories of the town and SCP-4458.

This feels…a little extreme

"Oh no, our town found a single anomalous shark in the sewers"
"Oh god, amnestize the town. AMNESTIZE THE TOWN. BURN HARMONY DOWN WE CANNOT LET THIS GET OUT."

Tone it down quite a bit. Relocation is fine, but give the example of a wandering serial cannibal that came from out of town. Amnestize anyone involved.

Gamma-6-31|Brown: Don't be so worried. That tank's made of, like, Foundation-brand maxiglass, or whatever. Something like 500,000 newtons to break it.

I'm not sure you quite realize how powerful 500k newtons is. Thats the maximum pulling force of a of a single medium sized locomotive. I understood this thing was powerful, but thats a bit much for me.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hello, thank you for the feedback. I changed the way SCI is formatted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The 𝒩ness article calls it an objective measure, but my thinking is that even if it's just Fisk's opinion, it kind of is objective with respect to Fisk. Like, "I like Star Wars" is a subjective opinion, but "My opinion is that Star Wars is good" is an objectively true statement. Ergo, any given thing is objectively good or bad in Fisk's opinion.

It's a valid interpretation, though!

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Deletion Vote
item.content:encoded: III

item.title: Staff Post - Deletion Vote
item.content:encoded:
Beginning deletion vote at -10.

As of 5:00 PM EST on 11/22/2019, the article is at -6 votes and ineligible for deletion. Timer removed. ~ Jacob Conwell

If you are not the author and you want to rewrite this article, you may reply to this post asking for the opportunity to do so. Please obtain permission from the author (or the Rewrite Team if this article is older than 6 months) and make sure you copy the page source to your sandbox. Please do not reply to this post for any other reason unless you are staff.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not liking science-y stuff is a fair opinion to have, but I personally disagree. As far as 𝒩ness goes, my personal headcanon is that, at least for the version of it used in this article, it's an objective measure that has nothing to do with Pisk.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the concept behind this (enough to upvote), but I can't help but feel like we've missed our chance at "Murders in the Rue Morgue, but with shark."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: i loved this scp mainly because of the strait forwardness and how it was mainly based on being a document and can you please maybe try to explain why the shark grows and shrinks. Also i gave this article a +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's not core to the object, but since it's the debut, I'm gonna say I'm not big on the Supplementary Classification Index. I'm only just warming up to the disruption/danger level stuff that's catching on. I feel like it's downright boring for the Foundation to understand anything mysterious or paranormal in this much depth (the "we're not actually sure why this happens" bit is present in just about every SCP whether it's stated or not), and actual "sciencey technical stuff" should only be included if it aids realism or atmosphere, because real science documents are boring.

Especially 𝒩ness. Isn't 𝒩ness literally "the subjective value of something in the opinion of one now-dead civilian with a serious inferiority complex"? (I guess Damien Pisk liked shark movies less than scientists, but more than criminals.) I see why the Foundation might measure it just 'cuz, but it's not the kind of thing that would appear in a need-to-know header.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Adding onto this: adding tags yourself is fine. However, please don't do it unless you're absolutely sure that you know what you're doing, lest you draw the ire of Tag Team.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I appreciate you're evoking low-budget shark movies as a genre, which is a phenomen that has always fascinated me. But the article doesn't really do anything with that; there's no subversion or tribute to the genre that can be interpreted here. It's just the Wikipedia summary for a trashy shark movie translated into an SCP document.

Also, cool with the SCI? I'd prefer if it were formatted better. ACS isn't intrusive, because it's a banner, but SCI is like a list of bullets that look unappealing in the context of the document.

Edit: Also, uh, coming from someone with an active C&D for the same thing, I'm 99% sure this article's tags are a violation of the tagging policy since you applied them yourself and "shapeshifter" is not a valid tag.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I enjoy this concept- it's amusing ! Although, I think there are some things that could be added to the article. I'd have liked to see some audio logs from Incident 4458-1, or perhaps an interview with one of the survivors.
All in all, +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Still just a Super Scary Killer Shark. It moves me to nothing.

Still has some description in the containment procedures.

Still has some containment procedures in the addendum.

You got rid of the rage state and made it clear how the shark gets out of the house once it's in. Well done on that. But I still have some trouble with the object and its presentation.

-1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A valid criticism. I didn't want to attach footnotes to those terms because it felt too cluttered, considering the SCI already had two footnotes already. If you're interested in what they mean, I explained it a few posts down.

Edit: I think this is a good enough criticism that it warrants adding the footnotes anyway.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Jaws is here!
Here is Jaws!
(He is a shark without a cause)
He was in a movie, you should watch it it's called Jaws

A shark-based SCP that I decided to use as the debut for the Supplementary Classification System. For those interested, "Creature" refers to an object that's an organic, non-sapient organism, and "Somatic" means the creature has physical effects. (As opposed to, for example, mental/sensory effects.)

The idea for this one is based off of something my dad told me a while back. Basically, his parents took him to see Jaws when he was about 6 years old, and it scared the crap out of him. He started thinking that the shark was everywhere, not just in the ocean, but in the pool, and even in his toilet. I thought that it'd be a fun thing to turn into a skip. Also, it gives me an excuse to link Lemon Demon's "Jaws" ;P

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I couldn't really think of anything other than the two addenda I added. Any sort of interview/mission log would break my goal of making this a purely document-based skip without dialogue, so I wasn't sure of what other way to go with it than the incident report.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A fairly good article. Not too much information, short and nice. I like the idea, and the writing is also very good, so it's an easy +1 from me.
—I also really like the supplemental classifications log, maybe this can become a new standard in biological SCPs.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: My reasoning for including Humes is that I see this skip as a low-level reality bender. It's able to manipulate its size at will with theoretically no bound, so while it can't use its powers on the world around it, it's definitely bending reality, at least on its own physical body.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really like the idea here and I can see this working while being light on story but the way this unfolds feels very unsatisfying and it breaks my suspension of disbelief that they would capture this in a glass container and then all get eaten.

Yeah; this largely mirrors my feeling here. There's definitely an interesting idea here but the way it's been explored feels very…flat? surface level? There's not enough here to really make me feel anything other than underwhelmed.

…also I kinda wish this was an SPC thing cos damn a size changing shark getting punched sounds fun.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I genuinely think adding the quantifiable measurements in an SCP document is a good idea and true to what an actual containment article in universe would contain, but I cant help but question the measurements here, especially Hume, which measures the difference in a space between baseline reality and I feel doesnt fit this anomaly at all. Still, I'm upvoting because I think this is an addition to the format I'd like to see expanded upon and used

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I am a strong contender of innovations on the format but I literally do not know what somatic, the hume level, the N ness or the faith counter means in this context. They are random numbers/terms that do not add anything to the experience or my understanding.

I am of the strong opinion that anything that does not add subtracts. And I feel like there is a lot of subtraction due to that here.

I really like the idea here and I can see this working while being light on story but the way this unfolds feels very unsatisfying and it breaks my suspension of disbelief that they would capture this in a glass container and then all get eaten.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: And thank you for responding. Upon reread, I approve of some of the extras for SCI.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks for the feedback. I tried to make this skip less story-focused and more of a pure document-based article since my last one was very story-based, and I wanted to try something different. The inclusion of 𝒩ness and Humes also isn't necessarily supposed to add something, it's sort of there just to show what can be in SCI other than object/anomaly class.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: SCI is a really interesting thing to add, and I approve of your boldness. +1 for me, also because sharks are cool.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's okay I guess. While the imagery and incident were fun, the story-department feels a bit lacking, and I'm still confused on the inclusion of 𝒩ness as a measurement, since it doesn't really add anything. +1, but I feel like it could be improved.
